title: What this section is for
paras: Let me start by telling you what this section is for. Most planning publications explain how the system works today. Very few say, honestly, where it's going — and fewer still separate what genuinely helps a planner do the job from what's being sold. This section exists to do that second thing. Its promise to you is credibility about the future: a grounded picture of how digital technology is reshaping planning, told by a practitioner rather than a salesperson, and disciplined by a single test that runs through every article that follows.

The test is maturity. Everything discussed here goes into one of three plain categories. In use means established and working in practice today — you can point to planners using it now. Emerging means actively trialled or in early adoption: real, funded, running somewhere, but not yet mainstream. And on the horizon means plausible, and grounded in technology that already exists or is in active development, but not yet delivered at scale. Alongside maturity sits planning impact — high, medium or long-term — because a technology can be real without mattering much to a decision, and it can matter enormously while still being years away.

The reason to be strict about this is trust. A great deal of planning technology marketing presents an on-the-horizon idea as though it were in use, and practitioners have learned to discount the whole category as a result. The honest position is the opposite of a sales pitch. Some of what follows is working now and worth adopting. Some is promising but unproven. And some is a reasonable bet that may not arrive on the timetable its advocates suggest. Saying which is which is the entire point.

title: Where things stand — as at July 2026
paras: So where do things stand, as at July 2026? This field moves quickly, so let's start with the live position. The Government has a live Digital Planning Programme in M-H-C-L-G, and its visible output is a push toward standardised, machine-readable planning data. The platform at planning dot data dot gov dot U-K now publishes national datasets — designations, constraints, local authority boundaries and more — under open licence. And the direction of travel is that plans and constraints become data a machine can read, rather than P-D-Fs a person must interpret. I'm certain of all that.

Two Government A-I tools moved from talk to delivery this year. The first is called Extract, built by the Digital Planning Programme with the D-S-I-T Incubator for A-I. It went live to all English local planning authorities on the 17th of June 2026. It turns historic planning documents — conservation area maps, Article 4 directions, tree preservation orders — into structured, standardised data, cutting a task that took up to two hours down to around two minutes. Within weeks, around fifty councils had processed over a thousand documents with it. Again, I'm certain of that. Separately, there's an A-I decision-support prototype — funded by M-H-C-L-G and built with the D-S-I-T Incubator, Google DeepMind and Faculty — which entered alpha in May 2026 with three volunteer authorities: Dorset, the London Borough of Camden and the London Borough of Barnet. It reads an incoming application, identifies the relevant policies and constraints, and helps the officer decide faster and more consistently. It targets householder and similar applications — roughly seventy per cent of officer workload — with an ambition to halve determination from eight weeks to four. The Government is explicit that, and I quote, the planning officer remains the key decision maker, and that the tool is not designed to replace the judgement an officer brings. If the alpha succeeds, it's due to expand to nine further authorities for a twelve-month trial, with wider rollout possible in 2027. I'm certain that's its status now — but the rollout is only likely, because it depends on the trial.

Funding continues through the PropTech Innovation Fund. Its latest round put around two point four million pounds into a dozen or so local-authority pilots covering plan-making and section 106 management — A-I-assisted application processing, automated summarising of consultation responses, and first-pass site assessment against constraints. That's certain.

On the statute book, the Planning and Infrastructure Act 2025 — Royal Assent on the 18th of December 2025 — sets the reform frame within which the digital work sits. Most visibly, that's through the National Scheme of Delegation, which standardises which decisions officers take and which go to committee. And that's a change that only pays off if the underlying data and casework systems are consistent enough to support it. I'm certain the Act and the Scheme exist; how far the digital ambition around them is realised, I'd rate as likely rather than certain. And on professional use, the R-T-P-I's position, from June 2025, is the sober one: A-I, in their words, must support, not replace, planners' local discretion, and is no substitute for professional advice, common sense, and emotional intelligence.

title: The state of play — maturity and impact
paras: Now for the state of play — maturity and impact. What follows classifies the technologies this section returns to. Maturity is judged on adoption in English planning practice specifically, not on whether the technology exists in the abstract — several of these are mature in other fields but early in planning. And treat the impact ratings as a view, not a fact.

Let me take them in order. First, A-I-assisted planning research. That's in use, and the impact is high. L-L-M tools already draft, summarise and answer policy questions in daily practice — fast, and fallible, so the planner checks and owns the output. Second, G-I-S-based decision-making. In use, impact high. Spatial data and constraint mapping are standard in development management and plan-making — the base layer everything else builds on. Third, drone surveys. In use, impact medium. They're routine for site survey, roofs, tall structures and inaccessible land, and they feed condition surveys, design and access statements, and enforcement evidence. Fourth, LiDAR terrain data. In use, impact medium. There's whole-England one-metre open coverage, and it reveals earthworks and ground levels for heritage, terrain and flood work. Fifth, digital public consultation. In use, impact medium. Online portals and interactive maps are now normal — the live question is the quality of engagement, not availability. Sixth, automated application validation. Emerging, impact high. Early tools check submissions against requirements before they reach an officer, cutting invalid applications and delay. Seventh, machine-readable planning policy and data. Emerging, impact high. Planning dot data dot gov dot U-K and the digital-plans work exist, but coverage is partial and fragmented — the payoff is real but not yet complete. Eighth, A-I-assisted decision support. Emerging, impact high. The Government prototype is in alpha — Dorset, Camden and Barnet — reading constraints and policy to speed officer decisions, with the officer still deciding. Ninth, digital or live local plans. Emerging, impact high. Plans as queryable data keyed to a site, not static documents — in development, not yet the norm. Tenth, immersive and A-R visualisation. Emerging, impact medium. V-R, A-R and accurate visualisations improve consultation and design review, but adoption is uneven and often bespoke. Eleventh, climate and environmental intelligence. Emerging, impact medium. Flood, overheating, carbon and ecology datasets feed assessment, and this is strengthening fast as the data improves. Twelfth, satellite monitoring. Emerging, impact medium. Repeat imagery supports enforcement and implementation checks — useful, but not yet systematic. Thirteenth, digital twins. On the horizon, impact medium. Authority-scale models to test daylight, overshadowing, flood and transport effects — real in pockets, not at scale. Fourteenth, BIM in planning. On the horizon, impact medium. It's standard in construction, but feeding structured building data into planning decisions is still early and inconsistent. Fifteenth, smart cities and sensor networks. On the horizon, and the impact is long-term. Real-time urban data could inform policy and monitoring — grounded, but diffuse and slow to touch day-to-day casework. And sixteenth, autonomous transport. On the horizon, impact long-term. It will reshape parking, highways and layout standards eventually, but it's too early to plan around with confidence.

title: What planners can realistically look forward to
paras: So what can planners realistically look forward to? Set aside the hype and a credible near-term picture emerges — every part of it tied to technology that already exists or is in active development. The first is the A-I planning assistant that answers a policy question in seconds. What does the development plan say about this, and what are the constraints on this site? It draws on machine-readable policy and constraint data. It's arriving now, and its value is speed of research — the decision, and the responsibility for it, stay with the planner. I'd call that likely.

Close behind is automated pre-submission validation: the system checks an application against the authority's requirements before a human sees it, so incomplete submissions are caught at source rather than weeks later. Coupled with that is the shift from the flat P-D-F to the intelligent, structured application — data an authority can read, sort and cross-check automatically instead of re-keying. Both reduce the administrative drag that consumes so much of an officer's week. Likely, in my view.

Then there's national constraint mapping — the ambition of selecting a site and seeing every designation and constraint that touches it, drawn from national data. The honest caveat is that the data largely exists but is fragmented. It's held by different bodies, in different formats, with gaps — so the experience is improving rather than finished. I'd say likely on the direction, but I'm frankly guessing on how soon coverage is complete enough to rely on unquestioned. Alongside it sit real-time performance dashboards for authorities and applicants, and live local plans that answer what applies to this site as data, rather than as a document to be read cover to cover. Likely again.

More ambitiously, digital-twin authorities would let an officer test a scheme's daylight, overshadowing, flood and transport effects against a model of the area before committing to a view. Immersive and comparative visualisation would let the public see a proposal in place and compare options — which is as much a democratic gain as a technical one. Environmental intelligence — protected-species records, flood and ecology data on demand — is tightening the evidence base. And predictive infrastructure planning — school places, G-P demand, utility capacity — could shift plan-making from reactive to anticipatory. Furthest out, planning knowledge graphs that reason over connected concepts rather than matching keywords, and autonomous site monitoring by satellite and drone for enforcement and implementation, are grounded but not yet delivered. The nearer items above I'd rate likely; on the timing of these last two, I'm guessing.

title: Spatial intelligence — the convergence
paras: Now to spatial intelligence — the convergence. Pull these threads together and they point one way. Digital planning, artificial intelligence and geospatial data are converging on what's best called spatial intelligence: systems that don't merely display map layers, but understand how constraints and datasets interact. The destination is a planner selecting a site and receiving an evidence-based first-pass assessment — planning history, designations, flood risk, ecology, heritage, access, utilities, demographics, comparable schemes — assembled in minutes, from sources that today take days to gather by hand. I'd call that likely.

Two things must be said plainly. The first is what it changes: it compresses the time spent gathering evidence, not the judgement applied to it. A first-pass assessment is a faster start, not an answer. The weighing of harm and benefit, the reading of context, the decision — those remain professional acts. The second is what it is not: it's an industry direction and a concept, not a product to be bought. No single platform delivers it today, and this section doesn't endorse any that claim to — including PlanRef itself. The value in naming the convergence is to help planners recognise the pattern behind a dozen separate tools, so they can judge each one against where the whole is heading. Again — likely, not certain.

title: The responsibility that does not move
paras: Which brings me to the responsibility that does not move. Everything above accelerates research and analysis. None of it moves the accountability. The planning judgement, the professional duty and the decision remain with the qualified planner, and a machine output is evidence to be checked, not an answer to be adopted. That distinction is the difference between using these tools well and being used by them.

The risks are specific, and worth naming. Hallucination: a language model can state a policy, a case or a figure that is plausible and wrong — so anything load-bearing must be verified against the source. Over-reliance: the more fluent the output, the greater the temptation to skip the check, and it's the professional, not the tool, who carries the consequence of a bad decision. Data currency and quality: an assessment is only as good as the data under it, and planning data is uneven and ages quickly — a confident answer built on a stale constraint layer is a confident error. And then there's the category mistake of treating a first-pass machine assessment as the assessment, when it's the beginning of one. Used with those risks in mind, the tools are a genuine gain. Used without, they're a liability wearing the costume of rigour. That's my view, and I'd rate it likely rather than certain.

title: Why it matters for the profession
paras: Why does this matter for the profession? Because the planner's skillset is shifting, and understanding these technologies is becoming part of the job rather than an optional extra. The work is moving toward data interpretation, A-I-assisted research used critically, G-I-S literacy, visualisation, fluency with environmental data, and multidisciplinary collaboration with the data and digital specialists now sitting alongside planners. None of this displaces planning judgement. It changes the raw material judgement works on, and it frees time from assembly for the harder, more valuable work of weighing it.

The reason to engage now isn't novelty — it's leverage. The planner who understands what a tool can and can't do gets the speed without inheriting the errors, and can tell a client or a committee why a machine-generated figure is, or isn't, to be trusted. That's a professional advantage, and it's available to anyone willing to treat these tools as instruments of the craft, rather than as either salvation or threat. The future of planning is not a machine that decides. It's a planner who decides faster, better informed, and just as accountable. Likely, in my judgement — but that's the direction.

title: Practice points
paras: Let me finish with some practice points. The first: classify before you trust. Ask of any piece of planning tech — is it in use, emerging, or on the horizon? Don't let an on-the-horizon idea be sold to you as though it were working now.

Second: the decision stays human. A-I accelerates research and analysis; the planning judgement, the accountability and the decision remain with the qualified planner. A machine output is evidence to be checked, not an answer.
Third: verify anything load-bearing. Language models hallucinate plausible policies, cases and figures. Check every fact you'd rely on against its primary source before it goes in a report.

Fourth: Extract is live now. Since the 17th of June 2026, every English L-P-A can use the Government's Extract tool, free, to turn historic planning documents into structured data — minutes instead of hours. That's certain.

Fifth: A-I decision support is an alpha, not a rollout. The M-H-C-L-G prototype is being trialled by Dorset, Camden and Barnet on householder cases, with the officer still deciding. Wider use depends on the trial — the status is certain, the rollout only likely.

Sixth: national constraint data exists, but it's fragmented. Planning dot data dot gov dot U-K and the digital-plans work are real and improving, but coverage is partial. Treat select a site, see every constraint as a direction, not a finished product.

Seventh: watch data currency, not just availability. An assessment is only as good as the layer under it. A confident answer built on a stale dataset is a confident error — check the date of the data, not only the output.

Eighth: spatial intelligence is a concept, not a purchase. The convergence into first-pass site assessment is an industry direction; no single platform delivers it today, and none should be adopted on that claim alone.

Ninth: the skillset is shifting. Data interpretation, G-I-S literacy, visualisation, environmental data, and A-I-assisted research used critically are becoming core planning skills, not optional extras.
Tenth: money follows the practical, not the visionary. The PropTech Innovation Fund pilots — validation, consultation summarising, first-pass site assessment — show where real value is landing first.

And eleventh: re-check this article. This is the fastest-moving area in the profession, and every dated statement here is as at the 19th of July 2026. Confirm the live position before relying on any of it on a real matter.

title: Sources
paras: A word on sources, if you want to go to them directly. The first is M-H-C-L-G Digital — Extract is here: A-I-powered planning data for every council in England, published the 17th of June 2026. That covers Extract's launch to all English local planning authorities, what it does, and the early adoption figures.

Next, M-H-C-L-G Digital again — Using A-I to support planning decisions: what it means for planners and residents, from the 19th of June 2026. That's the A-I decision-support prototype: its alpha status, the three trial authorities, the householder focus, the eight-week-to-four-week ambition, and the position that the officer remains the decision maker.

Then GOV dot U-K — A-I tool to speed planning decisions, from June 2026 — and Google DeepMind's piece, Unlocking U-K house-building with A-I-accelerated planning. Those give you the delivery partners — the D-S-I-T Incubator for A-I, Google DeepMind and Faculty — and the programme's stated aims.
Then planning dot data dot gov dot U-K itself — the national, open, machine-readable planning-data platform, and the direction toward standardised constraint datasets.
M-H-C-L-G's PropTech, or PlanTech, Innovation Fund — the latest round, 2026. That's the public funding for local-authority digital pilots in plan-making and section 106.

The Planning and Infrastructure Act 2025 — Royal Assent the 18th of December 2025 — together with M-H-C-L-G guidance on the National Scheme of Delegation, from 2026. That's the statutory reform frame and the standardisation of officer-versus-committee decisions.
The R-T-P-I — A-I tools for planning must support, not replace, planners, from the 9th of June 2025. That's the professional-body position on responsible A-I use.
And the Environment Agency National LIDAR Programme — whole-England, one-metre, open coverage. That's the maturity and availability of LiDAR terrain data for planning use.
This was written as at the 19th of July 2026, for England. This section's standard is honesty about maturity — every status and date above should be re-checked before it's relied on.
